fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that argument parsing utility we've been talking about. Basically devs are tired of writing the same boilerplate token-walking code every time they spin up a new CLI tool — it's brittle, inconsistent, and nobody wants to maintain it. The idea is you declare your args once and the library handles the rest: figuring out what's optional vs positional, dealing with those weird concatenated short flag cases like we handled in the auth CLI last quarter, converting values to the right types, yelling at users when they do something wrong, and spitting out decent help text automatically.

The JSON-in/JSON-out adapter layer needs to stay totally separate from the core logic — same principle we used on the config service, keep the I/O plumbing away from the business rules. Output should be sorted bindings, one per line, and errors should be plain English with no runtime type names leaking through.

Also needs to handle subcommands (think git-style), mutual exclusion groups, and the double-dash end-of-options separator. The help/usage rendering has to be auto-generated but also overridable. There's some nuance around how greedy positionals yield tokens to later required ones — make sure that's solid. Reach out if anything's unclear, there are test cases in the repo that cover the edge cases pretty thoroughly.

A couple follow-ups from the questions that came in: if an argument has default='::suppress::', then that binding should be omitted from the parse result completely. The name should not show up at all in the output, not even as null. That only applies when we're talking about the default suppression sentinel though — if the user actually provides the argument on the command line, we bind it normally. Same deal for help text: help='::suppress::' means hide that argument from the help output entirely.

On the positional side, the nargs='*' case should be careful, not just vacuum up everything that's left. It needs to look ahead and leave enough operands for any later fixed-count or required positionals, then only take the surplus for the star positional. If there’s nothing available for that positional, it binds an empty list, or its configured default if one is set and no tokens were supplied at all.

For printing results, keep it exactly one line per binding, sorted alphabetically by name, in the format <name>=<value>. A null value renders as 'null'. A boolean renders as lowercase 'true' or 'false'. A list renders as '[a, b, c]' with elements separated by ', ' and nested lists nest as '[[a], [b]]'; an empty list renders as '[]'. An enumerated value renders as its display string. And for parse failures, the whole output is just a single line in the form 'error: <message>', with no host-language runtime type names sneaking into the message.

One more parsing nuance: tokens that look like negative numbers, like '-3' or '-1.5', should be treated as positional values, not option flags, unless there's an actual declared option whose flag matches that token exactly. If there is, parse it as that option; otherwise leave it available to positionals.

Also, on short options, we do want the fused form handled the same way we talked about before: if the parser sees a multi-character short token, expand it left to right. Each character that maps to a no-value action like store_true, store_false, count, append_const gets consumed as a flag, and the first character that needs a value takes the rest of that same token as its embedded value if there is one, otherwise it consumes the next token. So something like '-vvvv' with count action increments four times. Mutual exclusion checks still need to fire even if the conflicting options showed up inside one concatenated token. That short-option lexing behavior from Feature 4 needs to stay intact too: expanding fused short-option tokens left-to-right, handling embedded values, and applying count/append_const actions across repeated characters in a single token.

For enums, there are two separate converter behaviors. 'enum' matches against symbolic member names from {RED, GREEN, BLUE}. 'enumstr' matches against display strings from {SMALL, MEDIUM, LARGE}, with the special case that LARGE displays as 'X-LARGE', so the user has to pass 'X-LARGE' and the output should also render as 'X-LARGE'. If conversion fails for either one, the error should be in the form argument <flag>: could not convert '<value>' (choose from {members}).

And just reaffirming the separation piece since that came up again: the JSON stdin/stdout execution adapter should stay completely decoupled from the core parser domain logic. The adapter’s job is only translating JSON requests into idiomatic core API calls.

trigger_keywords: one of these arguments is required
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A mutex group with required=true raises an error if none of its members are supplied, with the message: 'one of the arguments <flag1> <flag2> ... is required'. If two members of the same mutex group are both supplied (even via concatenated short options), the error is: 'argument <second_flag>: not allowed with argument <first_flag>'. A mutex group with required=false simply allows at most one member without mandating any.

Subcommands support prefix matching: a unique prefix of a subcommand name is accepted as if the full name were supplied. If the prefix matches more than one subcommand name (or alias), the parser rejects it with an error listing the ambiguous candidates. Subcommands may declare aliases that are treated as equivalent to the canonical name. The chosen subcommand's name (or the matched alias) can be recorded in the result under a configured 'dest' key. If subparsers are declared but no subcommand token appears in argv the parser raises an error.

The usage line is auto-generated in the form: 'usage: <prog> [options...]'. Optional arguments appear as [-x X], required optionals without brackets as -x X, required mutex groups as (-a A | -b B), and positionals by name or metavar. A custom 'usage' field overrides the auto-generated line but ${prog} within it is replaced by the program name. The version string similarly replaces ${prog}. The help body includes: usage line, description, optional-arguments section (including the auto-added -h/--help unless add_help=false), named argument groups, and epilog. Each argument's default is appended in help text when default_help=true.

A bare '--' token (double prefix with no following characters) marks the end of options. Every token appearing after it in argv is treated as a positional operand regardless of whether it looks like an option flag or another '--'. These forced-positional tokens are assigned to the remaining positional arguments in declaration order.

trigger_keywords: parser wide default overrides argument default
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A parser-wide 'parser_defaults' object injects name→value defaults into the result. When a name appears in both parser_defaults and in a per-argument default, the parser_defaults value takes precedence over the per-argument default. These injected defaults are merged into the final result alongside the argument bindings.

trigger_keywords: append action with nargs star empty list
empty list appended as nested element
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When an argument has action='append' and nargs='*', supplying the flag with no following value tokens collects zero tokens, producing an empty list [], which is then appended as a nested element into the accumulator. So --foo --foo with nargs=* and action=append where --foo gets zero tokens each time yields [[],[]]. For nargs='*' without append, supplying the flag with no tokens binds an explicit empty list that overrides any configured default.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'usage', 'help', 'version'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: there are test cases in the repo that cover the edge cases pretty thoroughly
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ directory containing feature1 through feature11 JSON test files, each defining input parser configurations and argv vectors with their exact expected output strings

context_pointers.vague_reference: yelling at users when they do something wrong
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The error output contract defined in the Execution Contract section: a single line 'error: <message>' covering required-argument missing, too few operands, unrecognized tokens, invalid choices, conversion failures, mutex conflicts, and ambiguous abbreviations — all without host-runtime type names

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but I don't have enough context on that specific detail from my side — you'd need to dig into the existing test cases in the repo or check with the tech lead. The test files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ are the source of truth for exact behavior; if the test covers it, that's the spec.

feature10_subcommands.json.description: Dispatch to one of several named subcommands, each with its own arguments and its own defaults. The chosen subcommand's operands and defaults are merged into the result; subcommands may declare aliases; a unique prefix of a subcommand name is accepted while an ambiguous prefix is rejected listing the candidates; the chosen command name can be recorded under a configured destination; and omitting the subcommand, or placing it after the end-of-options separator, is reported as an error.

cases.expected_output: error: ambiguous command: cl could match clean, clone

feature11_usage_help_version.json.description: Render the parser's self-documentation as text. Covers the one-line usage summary (auto-generated from the declared optionals, required optionals, mutually-exclusive groups and positionals, and an overridable usage template with program-name substitution), the full help body (program usage, description, an optional-arguments section including the auto-added help entry, named argument groups and mutually-exclusive groups, optional rendering of each argument's default value, and a subcommand listing with aliases), and the version string with program-name substitution.

cases.expected_output: usage: app [-h] [-a A] -b B (-c C | -d D) file

usage: app [-h]

This is a parser.

optional arguments:
  -h, --help             show this help message and exit

This is epilog.

feature1_basic_store_parsing.json.description: Resolve a token vector against a parser that mixes optional arguments (named with a leading prefix character) and positional arguments, producing a set of name=value bindings. Covers: an optional that consumes one value, an optional with an optional value that falls back to a fixed constant when given with no value, a positional that greedily collects the remaining tokens; precedence of a parser-wide default over a per-argument default; a default sentinel that removes the binding entirely so the name is absent from the result; redistribution of positionals so a greedy collector yields tokens to later fixed-count and required positionals; and an optional appearing between positionals.

feature2_nargs_quantifiers.json.description: Control how many tokens an argument consumes via a quantifier: a fixed count N, an optional single value '?', zero-or-more '*', and one-or-more '+'. Demonstrates an optional-value argument falling back to its constant when present without a value and to its default when absent; a wrong fixed count being rejected; the empty-list semantics of '*' for optionals (an explicit empty list overrides any default, and under an append action an empty list is appended); and the '*' semantics for positionals (absent positionals yield an empty list unless a default is configured, and a configured default is preserved when no tokens are supplied).

feature3_store_const_actions.json.description: Bind values by action rather than by consuming operands: flag actions that store a true or false boolean, an action that stores a fixed constant when the flag appears, a counting action that yields how many times a (possibly repeated/concatenated) flag occurred, an append action that accumulates each occurrence's value into a list, and an append-constant action that appends a fixed constant per occurrence.

feature4_short_option_lexing.json.description: Lexing of single-prefix short options: several boolean/short options may be concatenated into one token, and a trailing option that needs a value takes the rest of the token as its embedded value (or, if empty, the next token); a unique prefix of a longer option name is accepted while an ambiguous prefix that could match several options is rejected; an alternative prefix character can be configured and still supports concatenation; and tokens that look like negative numbers are treated as values rather than options unless a matching numeric option is defined.

cases.expected_output:
1=true
2=3=x
3=null
c=[true, true, true, true]
f=x
ff=a

feature5_type_conversion.json.description: Convert raw string operands into typed values before binding them. Covers integer conversion (including across a list-valued positional), conversion to an enumerated value matched by its symbolic member name, conversion to an enumerated value matched by its display string (which may differ from the member name), and conversion to a boolean restricted to a configurable pair of accepted literals. A value that does not convert is rejected with a diagnostic naming the offending input and the set of acceptable values.

cases.expected_output: error: argument --c: could not convert 'PURPLE' (choose from {RED,GREEN,BLUE})

feature6_choices_validation.json.description: Restrict an argument's accepted values to a fixed set or a numeric range, validating each operand after type conversion. A value within the allowed set/range is accepted and bound; a value outside it is rejected with a diagnostic that names the offending value and lists the allowed choices (a brace-enclosed enumeration for a set, or a brace-enclosed 'low..high' span for a range). The same validation applies to embedded values of a multi-value argument.

feature7_argument_separator.json.description: A bare double-prefix token ('--') marks the end of options: every token after it is treated as a positional operand even if it looks like an option or a value. Tokens following the separator are assigned to the remaining positionals in order.

feature8_error_conditions.json.description: Surface user-facing parse errors as a neutral diagnostic line. Covers: a required optional that was not supplied; too few operands for required positionals (including fixed-count and one-or-more quantifiers); leftover tokens that match no argument reported as unrecognized; and, when automatic help is disabled, the help flag itself being reported as unrecognized.

feature9_mutex_groups.json.description: Group arguments so at most one may be supplied. A single member is accepted and bound; supplying two members of the same group is rejected naming the conflicting pair; a group may be marked required so omitting all of its members is rejected listing the group's members; and the conflict detection applies even when members are concatenated into one short-option token.

cases.expected_output: error: argument --bar: not allowed with argument --foo
error: one of the arguments --foo --bar is required
